So the code for the function canChange:

We need to generate the s_list and t_list.

So:

class Solution {
public:
    bool canChange(string start, string target) {
        vector<pair<char, int>> s, t;
        for (int i=0; i < start.size(); ++i) {
            if (start[i] != '_') {
                s.push_back({start[i], i});
            }
        }
        for (int i=0; i < target.size(); ++i) {
            if (target[i] != '_') {
                t.push_back({target[i], i});
            }
        }
        if (s.size() != t.size()) return false;
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i=0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (s[i].first != t[i].first) return false;
        }
        for (int i=0; i < n; ++i) {
            char c = s[i].first;
            if (c == 'L') {
                if (t[i].second > s[i].second) return false;
            } else if (c == 'R') {
                if (t[i].second < s[i].second) return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Now, what about time and space complexity?

Time complexity: O(n), since we process each string once, and then compare O(n) elements.

Space complexity: O(n) for the two vectors. Since in worst case, all characters are L or R, so O(n) space.

But for n up to 1e5, this is acceptable.

The code is as follows:

class Solution {
public:
    bool canChange(string start, string target) {
        vector<pair<char, int>> s, t;
        for (int i=0; i < start.size(); ++i) {
            if (start[i] != '_') {
                s.emplace_back(start[i], i);
            }
        }
        for (int i=0; i < target.size(); ++i) {
            if (target[i] != '_') {
                t.emplace_back(target[i], i);
            }
        }
        if (s.size() != t.size()) return false;
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i=0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (s[i].first != t[i].first) return false;
        }
        for (int i=0; i < n; ++i) {
            char c = s[i].first;
            if (c == 'L') {
                if (t[i].second > s[i].second) return false;
            } else if (c == 'R') {
                if (t[i].second < s[i].second) return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};

But wait, in C++, pair's first and second are public. So yes, we can access s[i].first and so on.

So code works.

So this should be the correct solution.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    bool canChange(string start, string target) {
        vector<pair<char, int>> s, t;
        for (int i = 0; i < start.size(); ++i) {
            if (start[i] != '_') {
                s.emplace_back(start[i], i);
            }
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < target.size(); ++i) {
            if (target[i] != '_') {
                t.emplace_back(target[i], i);
            }
        }
        if (s.size() != t.size()) return false;
        int n = s.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (s[i].first != t[i].first) return false;
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            char c = s[i].first;
            if (c == 'L' && t[i].second > s[i].second) return false;
            if (c == 'R' && t[i].second < s[i].second) return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution compares the sequences of non-underscore characters in both strings to ensure they match. It then checks if each 'L' in the target is not to the right of its position in the start, and each 'R' is not to the left. This ensures valid movement constraints. The approach is efficient with linear time and space complexity.
